sources.notes: Read the complete current statement, examples, walk semantics, and all constraints; H2 differs from H1 only by reducing the walk limit to n+4.

Read the full official H2 tutorial and complete reference implementation, including stable rank normalization, the selector path, small/large block preparation, even and odd schedules, and the n+4 count.

Actually read the complete independent write-up. It identifies the three path macros, odd-even phases, the two-block cycling construction for even n, and the four preparatory operations plus protected minimum needed for odd n.

Independently proved stable rank reduction, selector extraction, the block-rotation invariant, even and odd phase counts, and the n+4 bound. A path-level simulator checked all permutations through n=8, all arrays over values 1..3 through n=8, and 150 random arrays up to n=500 as part of a 56,214-case joint check. Every route was monotone with exactly 2n-2 moves and every H2 result had sorted ranks within n+4 walks.

annotation.summary: Perform the same grid-walk-induced swaps as H1, but construct at most n+4 monotone paths rather than 2n+4, and finish with the original array sorted nondecreasingly.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 10000, 2 <= n <= 500, 1 <= a_i <= n, and the sum of n^2 over tests is at most 2.5e5. Every output path has exactly 2n-2 moves, and H2 allows at most n+4 paths.
annotation.core_idea: Replace equal values by distinct stable ranks obtained by sorting (value,original_index); sorting the ranks is sufficient to sort the original values. A selector walk through increasing columns p_1<...<p_k extracts the values currently at those positions into prefix positions 1..k in order. In at most four preparatory walks, put rank 1 first, use one key walk to send it to the end without leaving rank n first, select n and 1 into the first two positions, then select all S={1,...,ceil(n/2)} into a prefix with rank 1 first; the rest form B. A key walk left-rotates the array while realizing one matching of adjacent compare-exchanges inside any chosen contiguous block. For n=2k, apply k key walks while S migrates from front to back and perform alternating odd-even phases only inside B; the layout becomes B,S with B sorted. Apply k more while B migrates and sort S, returning to sorted S,B. For n=2k+1, take |S|=k+1 and |B|=k: k+1 first-half walks sort B while S migrates, then k walks sort S without its already-minimum leading rank 1 while B migrates. The active block shifts left after every walk, so its physical pairing parity flips with its head. Thus exactly n sorting walks plus at most four preparation walks suffice.
annotation.complexity: O(n^2) time and O(n^2) output size per test; O(n^2) memory when paths are buffered, or O(n) auxiliary memory when streamed. The number of walks is at most n+4.
tricks: Stable-rank duplicate values so the construction may reason about one permutation.
Use a monotone selector path to extract any increasing sequence of positions into a prescribed prefix.
Exploit the key walk's unavoidable left rotation instead of undoing it after every sorting phase.
Partition ranks into a small prefix block and a large suffix block, sorting one while the other block cycles around it.
For odd n, keep rank 1 fixed at the front of the small block and sort only the other k small ranks in k phases.
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness uncertainty remains after reading the official code and Luogu derivation, reconstructing the even/odd invariants, and path-level exhaustive/random verification.

The official prose compresses the odd-n case to two additional operations; the exact protected-rank-1 argument and k+1 then k phase schedule were reconstructed from the reference code and independently checked.
algorithm/sorting/odd-even-transposition-sort is absent from the current registry and is proposed as a reusable candidate; applying this payload requires taxonomy approval and a WebUI translation.
proof_risks: Rank normalization must assign distinct stable ranks to ties and carry those ranks through swaps; merely compressing equal values to equal ranks would break the permutation-based preparation.
The selector lemma guarantees only the selected prefix order; the unselected suffix is unspecified and must never be assumed sorted.
Before selecting [n,1], rank n must be away from positions 1 and n, and before selecting all small ranks, rank n at position 1 ensures the first selected position contains rank 1.
For odd n=2k+1, the two sorting halves have k+1 and k walks, not k and k; the second half sorts S without its leading rank 1.
The active block's physical head decreases after every left rotation, so compare-pair parity must change with it.
n=2 bypasses the general four-walk preparation and must retain its dedicated boundary handling.

solution_variants.name: rank-block-cycling-odd-even-sort
solution_variants.summary: Stable-rank the array, use selector walks to form consecutive small and large rank blocks with rank 1 positioned for the odd case, then spend exactly n key walks cycling one block around the other while alternating odd-even comparison phases inside the stationary block.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^2) time and output, at most n+4 walks, and O(n^2) buffered or O(n) streaming auxiliary memory.

tags.evidence: Each block is sorted by alternating disjoint adjacent compare-exchange phases while the opposite block migrates around it; the exact phase count proves the n-walk sorting stage.
tags.description: Sort a sequence by alternating parallel compare-exchange phases on pairs (1,2),(3,4),... and (2,3),(4,5),.... Use when this phase schedule or sorting-network structure is essential, not for arbitrary adjacent-swap sorting.
tags.created_reason: Both CF2039H versions encode one odd-even comparison phase into each constrained grid walk; the broad algorithm/sorting tag does not expose this decisive reusable phase-based sorting method.

tags.evidence: The solution explicitly assembles selector and diagonal-tiled grid paths whose induced permutations first form the rank blocks and then sort them within the strict n+4 budget.

Throughout each half, one value class migrates from front to back while the other remains a contiguous moving window receiving one alternating compare-exchange phase; this block invariant is the key improvement over H1.
Even n uses two k-phase blocks, whereas odd n needs sizes k+1 and k, a protected minimum, and k+1 then k phases; physical pairing also flips as the active window head changes parity.
Stable ranks turn the input into a permutation whose lower and upper rank intervals can be selected into contiguous blocks and tracked exactly under cyclic movement.
The selector, singleton pass, compare-swap tile, and shifting active window must all be emitted as valid full-length monotone R/D paths with careful n=2 and odd-n boundaries.
